text: [COURT SCENE: formal gathering indoors]
speaker: [Stage direction]
notes: Opening court scene establishes order before the fall

text: [Scene at Gloucester's castle - domestic setting]
speaker: [Stage direction]
notes: Private conversation, no weather mentions

text: How sharper than a serpent's tooth it is / To have a thankless child!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear's emotional storm begins; figurative language of emotional weather

text: O, let me not be mad, not mad, sweet heaven!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear begins to lose control; external storm approaching

text: O, fool, I shall go mad!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear exits into the night; storm about to break

text: Blow, winds, and crack your cheeks! Rage, blow!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Peak storm - Lear's opening command to the elements

text: You cataracts and hurricanoes, spout / Till you have drench'd our steeples, drown'd the cocks!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear demands a deluge; explicit storm imagery

text: You sulphurous and thought-executing fires, / Vaunt-couriers to oak-cleaving thunderbolts, / Singe my white head!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Thunder and lightning explicitly invoked; cosmic rage

text: Crack nature's moulding moulds, all germens spill / That make ingrateful man!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear's rage becomes cosmic; storm as metaphor for destroying ungrateful humans

text: Rumble thy bellyful! Spit, fire! spout, rain!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear addresses the storm directly; literal precipitation and fire

text: O! that this too, too solid flesh would melt, / Thaw and resolve itself into a dew!
speaker: KENT
notes: Kent's poetic response to the storm; dissolving into moisture

text: Blow, winds, and crack your cheeks! Rage, blow! / You cataracts and hurricanoes, spout!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear's continued command to the elements; storm rages

text: Here's the place, my lord; good my lord, enter. / The tyranny of the open night's too rough / For nature to endure.
speaker: KENT
notes: Kent comments on the 'tyranny' of the open night; literal storm description

text: My wits begin to turn. / Come on, my boy. How dost, my boy? Art cold?
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear notices the cold; physical sensations of the storm

text: Thou thinkest 'tis much that this contentious storm / Invades us to the skin: so 'tis to thee; / But where the greater malady is fix'd, / The lesser is scarce felt.
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear addresses the Fool; 'contentious storm' invades to skin

text: Let the great gods, / That keep this dreadful pother o'er our heads, / Find out their enemies now.
speaker: FOOL
notes: Fool calls the storm 'dreadful pother'; gods command the weather

text: The king comes out of his madness to find no shelter; he rages with the weather
speaker: [Scene direction]
notes: Continuing storm; Gloucester plans rescue

text: Thou think'st 'tis much that this contentious storm / Invades us to the skin; so 'tis to thee
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear on the heath; storm invades skin

text: Unbutton here. [Tears off his clothes] / Come, unbutton here.—Off, off, you lendings! Come; / Unbutton, unbutton!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear strips in the storm; extreme weather reaction

text: Is man no more than this? Consider him well. Thou owest the worm no silk, the beast no hide, the sheep no wool, the cat no perfume.
speaker: LEAR
notes: Storm-driven madness; philosophical reflection amid weather

text: There is the sulphurous pit—burning, scalding, stinking, consumption! Fie, fie, fie! Pah, pah!
speaker: LEAR
notes: Storm-induced madness produces hellish visions

text: [Scene: Cornwall's castle—indoors, away from storm]
speaker: [Stage direction]
notes: Interior scene; storm rages outside

text: The foul fiend bites my back!
speaker: EDGAR
notes: Edgar's madness; storm-influenced visions

text: Pray, innocent, and beware the foul fiend!
speaker: EDGAR
notes: Storm brings out madness in both Lear and Edgar

text: [Scene: inside Gloucester's castle—warm, lit]
speaker: [Stage direction]
notes: Interior scene; cruelty indoors while storm rages outside

text: The worst is not / So long as we can say 'This is the worst.'
speaker: EDGAR
notes: Transition from storm; dawn breaking

text: [Edgar guides Gloucester on the heath; the storm has passed]
speaker: [Stage direction]
notes: Storm aftermath; journey continues in gray dawn

text: [Cordelia's retreat—peaceful, contained]
speaker: [Stage direction]
notes: Refuge from storm; Cordelia's compassion

text: There is a cliff, whose high and bending head / Looks fearfully in the confined deep.
speaker: EDGAR
notes: Gloucester's supposed cliff; atmospheric description

text: How fearful / And dizzy 'tis to cast one's eyes so low!
speaker: EDGAR
notes: Atmospheric vertigo; but the cliff is illusory

text: There's a knell, methinks, / Struck from the world of dead.
speaker: [description]
notes: Fog/mist imagery in the recovered scene

text: [Lear sleeps peacefully, awakens in calm safety]
speaker: [Stage direction]
notes: Lear recovers in Cordelia's care; calm after the storm

text: You do me wrong / To take me out o' the grave: / Thou art a soul in bliss; but I am bound / Upon a wheel of fire, that mine own tears / Do scald like molten lead.
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear's emotional pain expressed as heat/fire; recovery moment

text: [Outside tent—day breaking, clear skies]
speaker: [Stage direction]
notes: Military preparations in clear dawn

text: [The battle passes—forces move across open ground]
speaker: [Stage direction]
notes: Brief battle scene; weather not foregrounded

text: [The final tragedy unfolds indoors/pavilion tent—darkness of despair]
speaker: [Stage direction]
notes: The culmination; internal tragedy dominates

text: Come, let's away to prison: / We two alone will sing like birds i' the cage: / When thou dost ask me blessing, I'll kneel down, / And ask of thee forgiveness.
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear and Cordelia's moment of grace; birdcage as refuge

text: Cordelia, Cordelia! stay a little. Ha! / What is't thou say'st?—Her voice was ever soft, / Gentle, and low, an excellent thing in woman.
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear with dead Cordelia; softness contrasted with storm

text: And my poor fool is hang'd! No, no, no life! / Why should a dog, a horse, a rat, have life, / And thou no breath at all?
speaker: LEAR
notes: Lear's final despair; emotional darkness absolute
